summary: Kansas has passed legislation limiting welfare recipients to withdrawing only $25 per day from ATMs using their state-issued debit cards. Due to ATM mechanics requiring $20 increments and fees totaling $3.50 per transaction, this effectively creates a 17.5% tax on welfare benefits. O'Neil argues this policy is a punitive transfer of money from the poor to banks, making it even harder for welfare recipients to access their own benefits and pay their bills.
key_points: The $25 daily limit effectively becomes $20 due to ATM withdrawal increments
ATM fees ($1 state fee + $2.50 bank fee) create a 17.5% tax on each $20 withdrawal
Many welfare recipients lack bank accounts, forcing them to use fee-charging ATMs
The policy punishes poor people while enriching banks
money_quotes: So altogether, they're giving $3.50 for every $20 of their welfare benefits, which I'd characterize as a bank tax of 17.5%.
Because poor people don't need that money, never mind the convenience of paying their actual bills.
key_insight: Poorly designed welfare policies can function as regressive taxes that transfer money from the poorest citizens directly to financial institutions.
